title: Fuel your brain
description: Sleep, move, eat, repeat — the body work that powers the mind.
summaryPoints: Protect 8–10 hours of sleep — it's when your brain locks in the day's learning
Keep a steady sleep schedule instead of trying to 'catch up' on weekends
Move daily — even a short walk boosts memory, focus and mood
Feed your brain steady fuel: whole foods, protein, and water, not sugar crashes
Take real breaks and go easy on late caffeine — small habits compound

content.title: The hungriest organ you own
content.body:
Your brain is about 2% of your body weight — but it burns roughly **20% of your energy**. It's the diva of your whole system.

So here's the twist nobody tells you before exams: how you treat your **body** is how you treat your **brain**. Sleep, food, movement, water. Boring? Maybe. But it's the cheat code under every study tip you've ever heard.
content.imageAlt: A brain ringed by sleep, water and exercise icons

content.title: Sleep is the heavyweight
content.body:
While you sleep, your brain replays the day and **locks in what you learned** — that's called consolidation. It also flushes out waste built up while you were awake.

Teens need roughly **8–10 hours**. Pull an all-nighter before an exam and you wreck the exact thing you stayed up for: your recall, your focus, and your mood all tank.

content.prompt: Pulling an all-nighter before a big exam helps you remember more.
content.explanation: Sleep is when your brain consolidates memory. Skip it and you sabotage recall, focus and mood — the opposite of what you wanted.

content.prompt: Roughly how many hours of sleep does a teen brain need each night?
content.explanation: Teens need about 8–10 hours — right in the middle is 9. That's the window where memory and mood actually recharge.

content.prompt: What's the smartest move the night before?
content.context: Your exam is at 9am tomorrow. It's already 11pm and you feel mostly ready.
options: Stay up until 3am cramming every chapter again
Skip sleep and rely on energy drinks at the exam
Study until you fall asleep at your desk
content.explanation: A rested brain recalls and focuses far better than a crammed, exhausted one. Sleep IS studying.

content.title: The weekend 'catch-up' myth
content.body:
Tempting plan: run on 5 hours all week, then sleep till noon Saturday to 'pay it back.' Problem — **sleep debt doesn't fully repay**.

A **consistent** sleep and wake time beats the crash-and-binge cycle every time. And late-night screens? The light and the endless scroll push your sleep later. Wind down first, then bed.

content.prompt: Sleeping in on weekends fully makes up for a week of short nights.
content.explanation: Sleep debt doesn't fully repay. A steady schedule all week beats trying to bank sleep on Saturday.

content.instructions: One word makes this advice backwards. Tap it.
content.sentence: Late-night phone scrolling improves your sleep.
content.explanation: Screen light and stimulation delay sleep — and sleep is when your brain locks in the day’s learning.

content.prompt: Pick ONE brain-healthy habit to start this week — and say why it'd actually fit your life.
content.context: Not all of them. One. The one you'd actually keep.
content.explanation: One habit you'll keep beats five you'll quit by Wednesday. Small and consistent wins.

content.title: Move it to remember it
content.body:
You don't need a gym. Even a **short walk** pumps more blood to your brain and releases BDNF — a protein that helps neurons grow and connect.

The payoff: better memory, sharper focus, lower stress, and a better mood. Regular movement is even linked to **better grades**. Stuck on a problem? Walk around the block and let your brain breathe.

content.title: Steady fuel, steady focus
content.body:
Your brain runs on a **steady drip of glucose** — so feed it whole foods: protein, fruit and veg, healthy fats. And **water** — even mild dehydration dents your focus.

Watch the trap: a big sugary or heavy-carb snack right before studying spikes you, then crashes you mid-chapter. Caffeine in small amounts can sharpen you, but too much or too late steals the sleep you actually need.

content.prompt: Helps your brain, or hurts it?

items.text: A huge energy drink at midnight

content.prompt: Match each habit to what it does for your brain.
Even mild dehydration hurts your focus, so keep drinking ___ through the day.
content.hint: It's free and comes out of a tap.
content.explanation: Your brain is mostly water, and even being slightly low on it makes concentrating harder.

content.title: Breaks aren't slacking
content.body:
Chronic stress quietly shrinks your focus and memory. Pushing for six hours with zero breaks isn't grit — it's how your brain fries out.

**Real downtime** recharges you: friends, a walk outside, a hobby, time in nature. And the big secret — none of this works as a one-time hero move. It **compounds**. Small daily habits beat one heroic cram every single time.

content.prompt: Put together a brain-friendly study evening, in order.
steps: Eat a real meal with some protein
Study in focused blocks with short breaks
Put the phone away and wind down
content.explanation: Fuel up, work in breaks, wind down off-screen, then sleep to lock it all in. That's the full loop.

content.title: Exam-week wellbeing
content.intro: Five days of exams ahead. Every day you make one choice — and your Brainpower carries the weight. Treat your body right and walk into the finals sharp. Start at 60.

steps.situation: Monday night. You're done studying. What now?

options.feedback: Your brain spends the night locking in everything you reviewed. This is the move.

options.text: One more all-nighter to be safe
options.feedback: Recall, focus and mood all tank tomorrow. You studied — then erased it.
Screen light pushed your sleep later and shaved hours you needed.
A jolt now, a crash later, and trouble sleeping tonight. Net loss.
More blood flow, more BDNF — focus and mood snap back. Cheapest upgrade there is.

options.text: Power through, no break, for six more hours
options.feedback: No breaks means rising stress and a fried, foggier brain.

steps.situation: Wednesday. You skipped lunch cramming. It's 4pm.

options.feedback: An empty, dehydrated brain can't concentrate — you're studying on fumes.

options.text: Crush a candy bar and a soda
options.feedback: Quick spike, then a crash mid-chapter. Not the fuel you need.

options.text: Eat real food and drink water
options.feedback: Steady glucose and hydration bring your focus right back online.

options.text: Study six hours straight, no breaks
options.feedback: Chronic stress shrinks focus and memory. Burnout isn't grit.

options.text: Take a real break with friends or a walk
options.feedback: Downtime lowers stress and recharges focus. Breaks aren't slacking.

steps.situation: Friday, the night before your last final.

options.text: Wind down off-screen and sleep early
options.feedback: Rested brain, sharp recall. You walk in calm and ready.
Exhausted and jittery is the worst exam-day combo. The habits compound — both ways.

content.outro: See the pattern? No single heroic night saved you — the daily choices stacked up. That's how a healthy brain is built: small, steady, every day.
